en.extract:
The Chilean swallow (Tachycineta leucopyga) is a species of bird in the family Hirundinidae. It breeds in Chile and Patagonia, migrating north as far as Bolivia, Paraguay, and Rio Grande do Sul.


Taxonomy and etymology
The new genus Tachycineta was created for this group of swallows by German ornithologist Jean Cabanis in 1850. The current genus name Tachycineta, is from Ancient Greek takhukinetos, "moving quickly", and the specific meyeni commemorates Prussian botanist and collector Franz Meyen. Some consider the species name to be leucopyga, which is what the species name originally was. This species, along with the white-rumped swallow, T. leucorrhoa, form a superspecies. This species is monotypic.


Description
The Chilean swallow is about 13 centimetres (5.1 in) in length and weighs 15–20 grams (0.53–0.71 oz). It is glossy blue-black above and white below with a white rump. Its wings and tail are black, with white tips on its inner secondaries and its tertials. Their underwing coverts and auxiliaries are both grey. The Chilean swallow's bill and legs are black. The sexes are alike, and the juveniles are duller and browner.
It is similar to the white-rumped swallow, but lacks the white forehead of that species and has bluer upperparts and grey underwing-coverts. In addition to this, the breeding ranges of the two have almost no overlap.


Distribution
This swallow is native to Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Falkland Islands, Paraguay, and Uruguay. It is vagrant to South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands. This swallow can also be found in Peru, but its origin is uncertain. Its breeding range is from the Atacama Desert in Chile and most of Argentina to the southern tip of South America. It is a year-round resident to the northern part of its range, but it is known to be migratory in the southern part. It is not well known where this population winters, but it seems to winter in northern Argentina, southern Brazil, Uruguay, and possibly Bolivia.


Behaviour


Breeding
This species has been seen to nest in both artificial and natural cavities, usually near water. They are usually solitary, although they will occasionally nest in loose groups if nest sites are near each other. Their nests are made from mud or dry grass and are lined with feathers.
This swallow is a seasonal breeder. It usually breeds from September and sometimes early October, especially near the tip of South America, to February. In Chile, it usually raises two to three broods.
The Chilean swallow usually has a clutch of four to six white eggs. The eggs measure 17.8 mm–21 mm × 13 mm–14.3 mm (0.70 in–0.83 in × 0.51 in–0.56 in) and weigh around 2 g (0.071 oz).
After the breeding season, it is found that the Chilean swallow forms flocks.


Diet
These birds are insectivores, usually feeding on flying insects. They usually forage alone or in small groups. When foraging, their flight paths are low and direct.


Status
The Chilean swallow is evaluated to be least concern by the IUCN. The justification for this is the fact that the species is believed to have a range of about 2,180,000 km2 (840,000 mi2), its population is increasing, and the population is not believed to be small enough to be vulnerable under the population size criterion.


References

The '''Chilean swallow''' ('''''Tachycineta leucopyga''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Hirundinidae]]. It breeds in Chile and [[Patagonia]], migrating north as far as Bolivia, Paraguay, and [[Rio Grande do Sul]].

==Taxonomy and etymology==
The new genus ''Tachycineta'' was created for this group of swallows by German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]] in 1850.<ref name=cabanis>{{cite book | last1 = Cabanis | first1 = Jean| author-link = Jean Cabanis | title = Museum Heineanum: Verzeichniss der ornithologischen Sammlung des Oberamtmann Ferdinand Heine auf Gut St. Burchard vor Halberstatdt | publisher = Independently commissioned by R. Frantz | volume = 1| year =1850 | location = Halberstadt | page =48 | language = de| url =https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/196124#page/64/mode/1up|access-date=December 4, 2016}}</ref> The current genus name ''[[Tachycineta]]'', is from [[Ancient Greek]] ''takhukinetos'', "moving quickly", and the specific ''meyeni'' commemorates Prussian botanist and collector [[Franz Meyen]].<ref name=job>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url= https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher= Christopher Helm | location = London | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n253 253], 377}}</ref> Some consider the species name to be ''leucopyga'', which is what the species name originally was.<ref name="cornell"/> This species, along with the [[white-rumped swallow]], ''T. leucorrhoa'', form a superspecies. This species is monotypic.<ref name="hbw">{{cite web |last1=Turner|first1=Angela|editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=Josep |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=Andrew |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=Jordi |editor4-last=Christie |editor4-first=David A. |editor5-last=de Juana |editor5-first=Eduardo |year=2013 |title= Chilean Swallow (''Tachycineta meyeni'')|work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |url= http://www.hbw.com/species/chilean-swallow-tachycineta-meyeni |publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona |access-date=11 December 2016 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Description==
The Chilean swallow is about {{convert|13|cm|in}} in length and weighs {{convert|15|-|20|g|oz}}. It is glossy blue-black above and white below with a white rump.<ref name="cornell">{{cite web|title=Chilean Swallow (Tachycineta meyeni), Neotropical Birds Online|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/overview?p_p_spp=523276|last=Marion|first=Jonah Seth|date=2010|editor-last=Schulenberg|editor-first=Thomas S.|access-date=December 10, 2016}}</ref> Its wings and tail are black, with white tips on its inner [[Flight feather#Secondaries|secondaries]] and its [[Flight feather#Tertials|tertials]]. Their underwing coverts and auxiliaries are both grey. The Chilean swallow's bill and legs are black.<ref name="patagonia">{{cite web|title=Birding Patagonia - Chilean Swallow|url=http://www.birdingpatagonia.com/aves_en/index.php?p=productsMore&iProduct=61&sName=chilean-swallow|access-date=December 12, 2016|archive-date=December 20, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220103415/http://www.birdingpatagonia.com/aves_en/index.php?p=productsMore&iProduct=61&sName=chilean-swallow}}</ref> The sexes are alike, and the juveniles are duller and browner.<ref name="hbw"/>

It is similar to the [[white-rumped swallow]], but lacks the white forehead of that species and has bluer upperparts and grey [[covert (feather)|underwing-coverts]]. In addition to this, the breeding ranges of the two have almost no overlap.<ref name="hbw"/><ref name="cornell"/>
<gallery>
Tachycineta_leucopyga-perched.jpg|White belly, [[Chile]]
Tachycineta_leucopyga-rump.jpg|White rump patch, Chile
Chilean swallow (Tachycineta leucopyga) in flight Maipo.jpg|flying in Chile
</gallery>

==Distribution==
This swallow is native to Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, [[Falkland Islands]], Paraguay, and Uruguay. It is [[Vagrancy (biology)#In birds|vagrant]] to [[South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands]]. This swallow can also be found in Peru, but its origin is uncertain.<ref name="IUCN"/> Its breeding range is from the [[Atacama Desert]] in Chile and most of Argentina to the southern tip of South America. It is a year-round resident to the northern part of its range, but it is known to be migratory in the southern part. It is not well known where this population winters, but it seems to winter in northern Argentina, southern Brazil, Uruguay, and possibly Bolivia.<ref name="cornell"/>

==Behaviour==
===Breeding===
This species has been seen to nest in both artificial and natural cavities, usually near water. They are usually solitary, although they will occasionally nest in loose groups if nest sites are near each other. Their nests are made from mud or dry grass and are lined with feathers.<ref name="hbw"/><ref name="cornell"/>

This swallow is a [[seasonal breeder]]. It usually breeds from September and sometimes early October, especially near the tip of South America, to February. In Chile, it usually raises two to three broods.<ref name="hbw"/><ref name="cornell"/>

The Chilean swallow usually has a [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] of four to six white eggs. The eggs measure {{convert|17.8|-|21|x|13|-|14.3|mm|in|abbr=on}} and weigh around {{convert|2|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name="hbw"/><ref name="cornell"/>

After the breeding season, it is found that the Chilean swallow forms flocks.<ref name="hbw"/><ref name="cornell"/>

===Diet===
These birds are [[insectivore]]s, usually feeding on flying insects. They usually forage alone or in small groups. When foraging, their flight paths are low and direct.<ref name="hbw"/><ref name="cornell"/>

==Status==
The Chilean swallow is evaluated to be [[Least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]]. The justification for this is the fact that the species is believed to have a range of about {{convert|2180000|km2|mi2|abbr=on}}, its population is increasing, and the population is not believed to be small enough to be [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] under the population size criterion.<ref name="IUCN"/>

zh.extract:
白臀树燕（学名：Tachycineta leucopyga）为燕科树燕属的迁徙鸟类，分布于南美洲。
此种繁殖地是在智利与巴塔哥尼亞, 迁徙至玻利維亞, 巴拉圭, 南里奥格兰德州
。
